5 min read
1 hour ago

--

We often overload HTTP with meanings it was never designed to carry. Recent discussions about “200 vs 404” for searches, and the arrival of the new HTTP QUERY method (RFC 10008), are good reminders to separate protocol concerns from business-domain concerns.
In this post I want to briefly lay out my view: HTTP status codes belong to the protocol layer; business logic should sit behind a clear abstraction and not leak into 4xx/5xx.

2xx, 4xx, 5xx: What They Really Say
In a modern stack we have many layers between client and application code: WAF, API gateways, load balancers, service meshes, proxies, etc. All of these speak HTTP and all of them understand HTTP status codes.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated with ChatGPT (OpenAI).For them, status codes describe the health and correctness of the HTTP interaction, not your business rules:

• 5xx: The server (or a downstream) is not reachable or not in a state to process the request.

• 4xx: The server is alive and able to do work, but the request is malformed or not allowed (authentication, authorization, rate limiting, unsupported media type, invalid URI, etc.).

• 2xx: The server is reachable, the request is syntactically valid, authorization passed, and the request can be processed.
None of these say anything about whether your business operation “succeeded”. They say “the protocol worked and your request made sense (or not)”.
That’s exactly why load balancers, WAFs, API gateways, client libraries and observability tools can all safely act on status codes: they’re generic, protocol-level signals.

Where Business Logic Starts (and Why It Should Stay There)
Consider a search API:

• GET /users?city=Munich → the collection exists, the filter is valid, and the client is allowed to perform the search.

• The result set might be non-empty, empty, or even semantically “unhappy” (e.g. the user has no active subscriptions).
From an HTTP point of view, all of these are perfectly fine 2xx responses. The protocol cannot and should not decide whether “no results” is a business error for your product.
I see two common patterns:

• Encode business failures as 4xx (e.g. 404 for “record not found”, 422 for semantic errors).

• Always return 2xx and describe business outcomes in the response body (including “no data found”, “domain validation failed”, etc.).
Both have valid use cases, and there are trade‑offs. My main concern is not which pattern is “pure REST” or “nicer for the frontend”, but who is consuming the signal:

• A 4xx is interpreted by every intermediary as “client-side problem with the request”.

• A business-domain failure may be perfectly valid from the protocol’s perspective and should not trigger WAF rules, client retry suppression, or unhealthy-node marking on a load balancer.
Because of that, my preference is to reserve 4xx/5xx strictly for protocol-level and generic client-server issues (authN, authZ, rate limiting, malformed JSON, unsupported media type, etc.), and keep all business-domain semantics — typed error payloads, domain-specific codes and messages — inside the response body, behind an abstraction layer that keeps the business logic itself unaware of HTTP status codes entirely.
In other words: the server can return 200 OK while the body clearly states “no records found”, “business rule X failed”, or “this operation is not allowed by policy Y”. Whether that is shown as an error message, empty table, or a warning banner is the client’s responsibility.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated with ChatGPT (OpenAI).

GET vs POST vs QUERY for Search
The same principle applies to method choice, not just status codes: don’t let transport-level mechanics stand in for what an operation actually means. The second long‑running debate is “Should I use GET or POST for complex searches?”.
The theory is simple:

• GET is safe, idempotent, cacheable, and intended for retrieval.

• POST is not guaranteed safe or idempotent; it’s typically used for state changes.
In practice, once queries become complex (sorting, pagination, nested conditions, ranges, many filters), URLs grow too large and we hit real-world constraints:

• Many intermediaries don’t expect or forward a body on GET, and may ignore it.

• So we push everything into the query string.

• But URIs have length limits at different layers, ending in errors like HTTP 414 (URI Too Long).

• Different proxies and tools behave inconsistently, making behaviour hard to predict.
To escape these limitations, many systems quietly switched to patterns like:
POST /search
Content-Type: application/json
{
"status": ["SUCCESS", "FAILED"],
"startDate": "2026-01-01",
"endDate": "2026-06-01"
}Functionally this works, but semantically it’s misleading: this is a read‑only query, yet we are using a method whose semantics allow arbitrary state changes.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated with ChatGPT (OpenAI).🔗 RFC 10008 introduces the QUERY method to close this gap:

• Request body like POST, so complex filters fit comfortably into structured content.

• Safe and idempotent like GET, so retries, caching, and intermediaries can treat it as a read-only operation.

• Designed for exactly this use case: flexible, expressive search and query operations without abusing POST.
Example:
QUERY /search HTTP/1.1
Host: example.org
Content-Type: application/json
Accept: application/json
{
"status": ["SUCCESS", "FAILED"],
"startDate": "2026-01-01",
"endDate": "2026-06-01"
}Semantically this reads as: “Perform this query, do not change server state, and return the results.” That is much closer to what we actually mean for analytics, reporting, and complex search APIs. Worth noting: as a very new method, QUERY is not yet universally supported — check your browsers, HTTP clients, and intermediaries before relying on it in production.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated with ChatGPT (OpenAI).

Closing Thoughts
Standards exist for a reason: they let many independent components speak a common language. But no standard is perfect or complete. HTTP status codes make our lives much easier across tooling, observability, gateways, and clients — as long as we keep them in their proper place.
My view in short:

• HTTP status codes are protocol signals, not business-domain verdicts.

• Once the protocol is healthy and the request is valid and authorized, 2xx is a reasonable default, even if the business outcome is “no data” or “rule violation”.

• Business logic should be isolated behind an abstraction layer and express its own rich semantics in the response body, independent of transport.

• For complex, read‑only queries, QUERY gives us a standard, safe, idempotent alternative to the long‑URL GET hack and the semantically awkward POST /search.
As always in architecture, “it depends” still applies — but keeping the separation between protocol and domain clear gives you more predictable systems and better-behaved infrastructure around your APIs.
